claims.claim: LLM serving controllers that optimize raw tokens/sec tend to select aggressive concurrency and batch-size settings, achieving high throughput at the cost of elevated latency, memory pressure, out-of-memory events, and poor request fairness.

evidence_refs.quote: # Multi-Objective Throughput Reward for LLM Serving Controllers: Trading Peak Tokens/Second for Operational Safety

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: We investigate a multi-objective reward function that penalizes constraint violations alongside throughput, comparing it against throughput-only and throughput-plus-latency rewards in a synthetic GB10-like serving controller benchmark.

evidence_refs.quote: # Multi-Objective Throughput Reward for LLM Serving Controllers: Trading Peak Tokens/Second for Operational Safety

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Across 120 independent seeds and 120 epochs per seed (50-epoch tail analysis), the multi-objective reward reduced latency violation rates by 92.5%, low-memory violation rates by 92.7%, and OOM-event rates by 90.1% relative to throughput-only reward, while improving Jain fairness by 8.6% and evaluation utility by 24.7%.

evidence_refs.quote: # Multi-Objective Throughput Reward for LLM Serving Controllers: Trading Peak Tokens/Second for Operational Safety

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The throughput cost was 17.1% fewer tokens/sec.

evidence_refs.quote: # Multi-Objective Throughput Reward for LLM Serving Controllers: Trading Peak Tokens/Second for Operational Safety

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Seed-paired analysis confirmed a utility improvement of +0.115 absolute (95% CI [+0.102, +0.128]).

evidence_refs.quote: # Multi-Objective Throughput Reward for LLM Serving Controllers: Trading Peak Tokens/Second for Operational Safety

claims.claim: Throughput maximization is a natural objective for LLM serving controllers.

evidence_refs.quote: # Multi-Objective Throughput Reward for LLM Serving Controllers: Trading Peak Tokens/Second for Operational Safety

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: However, raw tokens/sec is an incomplete proxy for operational utility: aggressive concurrency and batch-size configurations that maximize throughput can simultaneously degrade tail latency, exhaust memory headroom, trigger out-of-memory errors, and distribute service unfairly across requests.

evidence_refs.quote: # Multi-Objective Throughput Reward for LLM Serving Controllers: Trading Peak Tokens/Second for Operational Safety

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The central hypothesis of this work is that a multi-objective throughput reward—one that trades some peak throughput for constraint satisfaction across latency, memory headroom, error rates, and fairness—will yield higher operational utility and far fewer constraint violations than a throughput-only reward, with an acceptable throughput penalty.

evidence_refs.quote: # Multi-Objective Throughput Reward for LLM Serving Controllers: Trading Peak Tokens/Second for Operational Safety

claims.claim: We operationalize this hypothesis as a reward-shaping experiment in a synthetic serving controller that selects (max_concurrency, batch) actions under a GB10-like resource profile.
